We can see the effect of many similar others in another remarkable study. This one led by my UK colleague Steve J. Martin, who was asked by the British government to help reduce the number of UK citizens who don't pay their taxes on time. Researchers who don't pay on time traditionally get threatening letters from Her Majesty's tax service, describing some nasty consequences of failing to pay in a timely way. That standard letter produced 67% compliance. Steve worked with British government researchers to add one sentence to the standard letter. It was a sentence that engaged the principle of social proof by stating accurately that the vast majority of UK citizens do pay their taxes on time, which increased payments from 67% to 72%. Note that this sentence employed the amplifier of the many by referencing the vast majority of UK residents. But the researchers also worked to tailor new letters that leveraged the similarity amplifier of social proof by sending out two additional test letters. One modified the new sentence to read that the majority of taxpayers in your region of the UK pay on time, which upped timely payments to 79%. The second test letter enhanced similarity even more by stating that the vast majority of taxpayers in your town pay on time, which produced an astounding 83% level of compliance. These small changes to the standard letter have become famous within Her Majesty's tax service because they have generated so much more in tax revenue. In fact, today the new letter is called the 5 billion pound letter because that's how much additional revenue it has generated across the UK ever since. All due to detailed knowledge of how to maximize the force of social proof. Talk about a small big.
